title: Encode handler emits metric names not declared in the CodecMetric discriminated union
description: postFinalMetrics() emits 'encode_create_ms', 'encode_push_pixels_ms', 'encode_wait_pixels_ms', 'encode_finish_ms', 'encode_chunk_yield_ms' (line 222-227). readEncoderChunks() additionally emits 'encode_chunk_yield_total_ms', 'encode_output_bytes', 'encode_time_to_first_byte_ms' (lines 427, 448-449). The CodecMetric union in jxl-core/src/types.ts only declares 'encode_total_ms' from this group; every other encode metric name falls outside the discriminated union. Any consumer that switches on CodecMetric.name exhaustively (scheduler, session) silently drops these metrics because they match no arm.

title: encode_chunk_yield_ms and encode_chunk_yield_total_ms are both emitted for the same value, creating a duplicate metric
description: readEncoderChunks() posts 'encode_chunk_yield_total_ms' with this.totalChunkYieldMs (line 448), then immediately calls postFinalMetrics() which posts 'encode_chunk_yield_ms' with the same this.totalChunkYieldMs value (line 227). Both metrics represent the same cumulative chunk-yield duration, but under two different names. Consumers receiving both will either count the work twice or one name will silently be ignored. The naming asymmetry ('_ms' vs '_total_ms') also breaks naming consistency with other metrics.

title: partialPixelStride is conditionally omitted when partialPixels is present, violating the protocol contract that declares it required
description: The protocol comment on MsgDecodeError.partialPixelStride (protocol.ts line 144) states 'required when partialPixels is present'. In failSession(), partialPixelStride is only set when the caller passes it as a non-undefined argument (line 603: 'if (partialPixelStride !== undefined) msg.partialPixelStride = partialPixelStride'). The error event path (readDecoderEvents line 555-563) correctly supplies partialPixelStride or calls pixelStrideForFormat() as fallback. However the function signature allows callers to pass partialPixels+partialInfo without partialPixelStride, which would produce a non-compliant MsgDecodeError. The contract violation is latent now but can be triggered if a new internal failSession() call omits stride.

title: EncodeHandler drain message always reports adaptiveHwm as the constant CHUNK_HWM, never the runtime value
description: The pre-allocated _drainMsg is initialized with 'adaptiveHwm: CHUNK_HWM' (line 86, value 4) and maybePostDrain() never updates this field before calling self.postMessage (lines 397-400). DecodeHandler correctly updates adaptiveHwm to the computed runtime value before each drain post (decode-handler.ts line 404: '_drainMsg.adaptiveHwm = hwm'). The scheduler reads adaptiveHwm from drain messages to tune its push high-water mark. Receiving a stale constant 4 instead of the actual computed HWM causes the scheduler to apply a fixed HWM for encode sessions rather than the adaptive value. MsgWorkerDrain declares adaptiveHwm as optional, so the field is structurally valid, but the value is semantically wrong.

title: pixelStrideForFormat does not handle 'rgb8' format and will return 4 (rgba8 default) for it
description: pixelStrideForFormat() returns stride values for 'rgba8' (4), 'rgba16' (8), and 'rgbaf32' (16) but falls through to the default '4' for any other value, including 'rgb8'. PixelFormat includes 'rgb8' as a valid decode output format. If an error event arrives with format='rgb8' and the codec omits partialPixelStride, the fallback will compute stride=4 (rgba8) instead of the correct stride=3. MsgDecodeError.partialPixelStride would then carry an incorrect value to the consumer, causing pixel data to be interpreted with a wrong row stride.

title: EncodeHandler accesses MsgEncodeStart fields via type cast to bypass missing type declarations, hiding schema drift
description: Multiple encoder options are accessed with '(this.opts as MsgEncodeStart).fieldName' casts (lines 174-187: progressiveDc, progressiveAc, qProgressiveAc, groupOrder, orientation, centerX, centerY, intrinsicSize, disablePerceptualHeuristics, codestreamLevel). These fields ARE declared in MsgEncodeStart in protocol.ts. The casts were introduced when the fields were not yet in the type but have since been added. The casts are now a misleading anti-pattern: they suggest the type doesn't include these fields, preventing TypeScript from catching future removals or type changes. More critically, progressiveFlavor uses '(this.opts as MsgEncodeStart & { progressiveFlavor?: ... })' even though MsgEncodeStart already declares progressiveFlavor?, masking any future type-narrowing errors.
code_excerpt:
progressiveFlavor: (this.opts as MsgEncodeStart & { progressiveFlavor?: "dc" | "ac" }).progressiveFlavor,
progressiveDc: (this.opts as MsgEncodeStart).progressiveDc,
progressiveAc: (this.opts as MsgEncodeStart).progressiveAc,
qProgressiveAc: (this.opts as MsgEncodeStart).qProgressiveAc,
groupOrder: (this.opts as MsgEncodeStart).groupOrder,
// ... orientation, centerX, centerY, intrinsicSize,
// disablePerceptualHeuristics, codestreamLevel — all cast when already in type

title: wasm.createDecoder is called with progressiveDetail conditionally spread, but the JxlModule interface always includes the field as optional — the conditional spread is unnecessary and confusing
description: At line 250, progressiveDetail is conditionally spread '...(this.opts.progressiveDetail !== null ? { progressiveDetail: this.opts.progressiveDetail } : {})'. The JxlModule.createDecoder interface declares 'progressiveDetail?' as optional, so passing 'undefined' or simply omitting the field would be valid. The null-to-absent transform is a contract artifact: opts.progressiveDetail is typed as '... | null' in MsgDecodeStart but the WASM interface uses 'undefined' absence. If opts.progressiveDetail is null, the spread correctly omits the key, but if some consumer checks the key with 'in' rather than truthiness they get inconsistent behavior across the null vs undefined case. The real contract gap is the null/undefined impedance mismatch between protocol types (null) and WASM facade types (undefined-absent).

title: routeDecodeMessage and routeEncodeMessage silently drop messages for sessions that have already ended
description: When a decode_chunk or encode_pixels arrives after the session handler has called onSessionEnd() and been removed from decodeSessions/encodeSessions, routeDecodeMessage() finds neither a live handler nor a pending start, so the message is silently dropped (no error reported). The caller (scheduler/session) cannot distinguish silent drop from clean delivery. This is especially problematic for decode_cancel messages sent after onSessionEnd: a cancel arriving 1ms late disappears silently, and the caller may block waiting for decode_cancelled that will never arrive. No contract states messages to ended sessions are dropped vs error-reported.

title: No contract test verifying that all CodecMetric names emitted by handlers are declared in the CodecMetric union
description: The handlers.test.ts suite tests behavioral invariants (message ordering, budget behavior, pause/resume) but no test checks that every metric name posted via postMetric() is a member of the CodecMetric discriminated union in types.ts. As shown by finding contracts-1, several encode metric names are currently undeclared. A simple test that collects all 'metric' messages from a full encode/decode run and asserts each name matches a CodecMetric member would catch future drift at the test layer rather than requiring a manual protocol audit.

title: MsgWorkerReady carries no protocol version field, so old workers and new hosts cannot detect incompatible message formats
description: MsgWorkerReady carries 'backend' and optionally 'wasmBuild' but no protocol version number. If message shapes change (new required field, removed field, semantic change to existing field), a stale worker loaded from cache will still send a valid-looking worker_ready and the host will accept it without detecting the mismatch. Adding a monotonically increasing integer 'protocolVersion' to MsgWorkerReady and MsgDecodeStart/MsgEncodeStart would let the host reject workers that speak an incompatible protocol version, preventing subtle misinterpretation of transferred buffers after a deploy.
